en.extract:
The black-fronted tyrannulet (Phylloscartes nigrifrons) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Brazil, Guyana, and Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The black-fronted tyrannulet was originally described in 1884 as Leptopogon nigrifrons. It was later transferred to genus Mecocerculus and then to its present genus Phylloscartes. Though relationships among members of Phylloscartes have not been fully resolved, it appears that the black-fronted tyrannulet's closest relatives are the rufous-lored tyrannulet (P. flaviventris) and the cinnamon-faced tyrannulet (P. parkeri).
The black-fronted tyrannulet is monotypic.


Description
The black-fronted tyrannulet is 12 to 13 cm (4.7 to 5.1 in) long; five individuals weighed 8 to 11.1 g (0.28 to 0.39 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black forecrown ("front"), lores, and area behind the lores which give it a frowning appearance. They have a thin white supercilium. Their face is otherwise grizzled white and blackish with a black crescent around the ear coverts. Their crown is dark gray and the rest of their upperparts are dark olive. Their wings are blackish with thin pale olive yellow edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are blackish with pale yellow to yellowish white tips that form two wing bars. Their long slender tail is olive. Their throat is mottled gray and white, their breast is pale gray, and their belly is pale yellowish white. Both sexes have a brown iris, a black bill, and gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The black-fronted tyrannulet is found primarily in Venezuela, on the tepuis in the states of Amazonas and Bolívar. Its range extends slightly into adjacent western Guyana and far northwestern Brazil. It primarily inhabits the interior and edges of humid montane evergreen forest and to a lesser extent mature secondary forest dominated by Melastomataceae. In elevation it ranges between 900 and 1,800 m (3,000 and 5,900 ft) in Venezuela and between 800 and 1,800 m (2,600 and 5,900 ft) in Brazil.


Behavior


Movement
The black-fronted tyrannulet is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The black-fronted tyrannulet's diet has not been detailed but is known to include insects, spiders, and small fruits. It forages actively, from the forest's lower story to its canopy. It typically perches horizontally on a branch, usually with its tail cocked up, and makes short sallies to hover-glean prey from leaves and twigs. It typically forages in pairs or small family groups and usually as part of a mixed-species feeding flock.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the black-fronted tyrannulet's breeding biology.


Vocalization
As of late 2024 xeno-canto had only eight recordings of black-fronted tyrannulet vocalizations; the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library had 18.
The black-fronted tyrannulet's song, which is infrequently heard, is "a long (2-3 sec.), very thin trill, [with the] last half slightly descending". While foraging it sings "a thin, sharp tsuk-cheez-tr'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r, 2nd note highest". While foraging it also makes a "cheh-cheh-ch'leet!" call.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the black-fronted tyrannulet as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common and "occupies a region that is remote and difficult to access, but there are reports of severe local effects within this region from gold prospectors and 'uncontrolled tourism' ".

The '''black-fronted tyrannulet''' ('''''Phylloscartes nigrifrons''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers.<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref> It is found in [[Brazil]], [[Guyana]], and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 18 November 2024. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 26 November 2024</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The black-fronted tyrannulet was [[Species description|originally described]] in 1884 as ''Leptopogon nigrifrons''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Salvin |first=Osbert |last2=Godman |first2=Frederick Du Cane | journal=Ibis | title=XLVI. Notes on Birds from British Guiana Part III | volume=II |number=VIII | page=446 |publisher=British Ornithologists' Union | date= 1884 | language=Latin, English |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8526807 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1884.tb01180.x |access-date=23 December 2024}}</ref> It was later transferred to genus ''[[Mecocerculus]]'' and then to its present genus ''[[Phylloscartes]]''.<ref name=BFTY-BOW>Schulenberg, T. S. and G. M. Kirwan (2021). Black-fronted Tyrannulet (''Phylloscartes nigrifrons''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.blftyr1.01.1 retrieved 23 December 2024</ref> Though relationships among members of ''Phylloscartes'' have not been fully resolved, it appears that the black-fronted tyrannulet's closest relatives are the [[rufous-lored tyrannulet]] (''P. flaviventris'') and the [[cinnamon-faced tyrannulet]] (''P. parkeri'').<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 18 November 2024. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved 26 November 2024</ref>

The black-fronted tyrannulet is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/>

==Description==

The black-fronted tyrannulet is {{convert|12|to|13|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long; five individuals weighed {{convert|8|to|11.1|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black forecrown ("front"), [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]], and area behind the lores which give it a frowning appearance. They have a thin white [[supercilium]]. Their face is otherwise grizzled white and blackish with a black crescent around the ear [[coverts]]. Their crown is dark gray and the rest of their upperparts are dark olive. Their wings are blackish with thin pale olive yellow edges on the flight feathers. Their wing [[coverts]] are blackish with pale yellow to yellowish white tips that form two [[wing bar]]s. Their long slender tail is olive. Their throat is mottled gray and white, their breast is pale gray, and their belly is pale yellowish white. Both sexes have a brown iris, a black bill, and gray legs and feet.<ref name=BFTY-BOW/><ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | page =585 | language =English }}</ref><ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =300–301 |isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The black-fronted tyrannulet is found primarily in Venezuela, on the [[tepui]]s in the states of [[Amazonas (Venezuelan state)|Amazonas]] and [[Bolívar (state)|Bolívar]]. Its range extends slightly into adjacent western Guyana and far northwestern Brazil. It primarily inhabits the interior and edges of humid montane [[evergreen forest]] and to a lesser extent mature [[secondary forest]] dominated by [[Melastomataceae]]. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|900|and|1800|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Venezuela and between {{convert|800|and|1800|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Brazil.<ref name=BFTY-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/><ref name=vanPerlo/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The black-fronted tyrannulet is a year-round resident.<ref name=BFTY-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The black-fronted tyrannulet's diet has not been detailed but is known to include insects, spiders, and small fruits. It forages actively, from the forest's lower story to its canopy. It typically perches horizontally on a branch, usually with its tail cocked up, and makes short sallies to hover-glean prey from leaves and twigs. It typically forages in pairs or small family groups and usually as part of a [[mixed-species feeding flock]].<ref name=BFTY-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/><ref name=vanPerlo/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the black-fronted tyrannulet's breeding biology.<ref name=BFTY-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Phylloscartes-nigrifrons |species=the black-fronted tyrannulet}}

===Vocalization===

As of late 2024 [[xeno-canto]] had only eight recordings of black-fronted tyrannulet vocalizations; the [[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]'s [[Macaulay Library]] had 18.<ref name=BFTY-BOW/>

The black-fronted tyrannulet's song, which is infrequently heard, is "a long (2-3 sec.), very thin trill, [with the] last half slightly descending". While foraging it sings "a thin, sharp ''tsuk-cheez-tr'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'r'', 2nd note highest". While foraging it also makes a "''cheh-cheh-ch'leet!''" call.<ref name=Hilty/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the black-fronted tyrannulet as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common and "occupies a region that is remote and difficult to access, but there are reports of severe local effects within this region from gold prospectors and 'uncontrolled tourism' ".<ref name=BFTY-BOW/>
